era_context: The early 1770s sat at the apex of Enlightenment intellectual ferment and the accelerating collapse of ancien régime stability across Europe and its empires. The Russo-Turkish War (1768–1774) was reshaping the Black Sea balance of power. Britain's North American colonies were moving toward open confrontation over taxation and representation. In East Asia, the Qing dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor remained the world's most populous state, while Tokugawa Japan maintained its sakoku policy. The Atlantic slave trade operated at peak volumes, the first Industrial Revolution was taking hold in Britain's textile industry, and Enlightenment reform movements were testing absolutist governance from Copenhagen to Moscow.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading states and Ethiopia
Central Asian steppe polities between Russian and Qing spheres

events.title: Russian Conquest of Crimea
events.description: In June–July 1771, a Russian army under Prince Vasily Dolgorukov invaded the Crimean Khanate during the Russo-Turkish War, rapidly overrunning the peninsula. The Crimean Khan Selim III Giray fled, and Russia installed the more pliable Sahib II Giray. This conquest effectively ended the Khanate's independence as an Ottoman vassal and foreshadowed the formal Russian annexation of Crimea in 1783. The campaign demonstrated Russia's growing capacity to project power southward toward the Mediterranean, alarming the European balance-of-power system.
key_figures: Vasily Dolgorukov (Russian general, commander of Crimean expedition)
Sahib II Giray (installed as Russian-backed Khan)

sources.name: Russian military dispatches and campaign records, 1771
Brian Davies, The Russo-Turkish War, 1768–1774: Catherine II and the Ottoman Empire (2016)
Alan W. Fisher, The Russian Annexation of the Crimea, 1772–1783 (1970)

events.certainty_note: Well documented in Russian military records and Ottoman correspondence. The campaign timeline is firmly established.

events.title: Moscow Plague Riot
events.description: A devastating bubonic plague epidemic struck Moscow from late 1770 into 1771, killing an estimated 50,000–100,000 people in the city. Quarantine measures, the closing of public baths, and restrictions on religious gatherings provoked widespread unrest. On 15–17 September 1771, a major riot erupted: mobs killed Archbishop Ambrose of Moscow, attacked quarantine houses and hospitals, and overran the Kremlin. The uprising was suppressed by troops under Grigory Orlov, dispatched by Catherine II. The crisis exposed the inadequacy of Russian urban public health infrastructure and prompted administrative reforms.
key_figures: Grigory Orlov (imperial envoy, suppressed the riot)
Archbishop Ambrose of Moscow (killed by the mob)
Pyotr Saltykov (Governor-General of Moscow, who fled the city)

sources.name: John T. Alexander, Bubonic Plague in Early Modern Russia: Public Health and Urban Disaster (1980)
Catherine II's correspondence regarding the plague and riot, 1771
Moscow Senate and police reports, September 1771

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in Russian imperial archives, personal correspondence, and official reports. Death toll estimates vary significantly but the riot itself is firmly attested.

events.title: Battle of Alamance — Suppression of the Regulator Movement
events.description: On 16 May 1771, North Carolina Royal Governor William Tryon led approximately 1,000 militia against some 2,000 poorly organized Regulators near Alamance Creek. The Regulators were backcountry settlers who had been protesting corrupt local officials, excessive taxation, and lack of representation since the mid-1760s. The battle lasted about two hours and resulted in a decisive government victory. Six Regulator leaders were subsequently tried and hanged. The movement is sometimes interpreted as a precursor to the American Revolution, though the Regulators' grievances were primarily local and many former Regulators remained Loyalists during the war.
key_figures: William Tryon (Royal Governor of North Carolina)
Herman Husband (Regulator leader, fled before battle)
Edmund Fanning (corrupt official, target of Regulator grievances)

sources.name: William Tryon's official dispatches to Lord Hillsborough, 1771
Colonial Records of North Carolina, vol. VIII–IX
Marjoleine Kars, Breaking Loose Together: The Regulator Rebellion in Pre-Revolutionary North Carolina (2002)

events.certainty_note: Well documented in colonial records and Governor Tryon's correspondence. Date and outcomes are firmly established.

events.title: Tây Sơn Rebellion Begins in Vietnam
events.description: In 1771, three brothers from the village of Tây Sơn — Nguyễn Nhạc, Nguyễn Huệ, and Nguyễn Lữ — launched a peasant rebellion against the ruling Nguyễn lords in southern Vietnam. The revolt began in the Bình Định highlands and drew on widespread discontent among peasants, ethnic minorities, and merchants oppressed by corrupt Nguyễn officials. The Tây Sơn movement would grow to overthrow both the Nguyễn lords in the south and the Trịnh lords in the north, briefly unifying Vietnam under Nguyễn Huệ (Emperor Quang Trung) and famously defeating a Qing Chinese invasion in 1789.
key_figures: Nguyễn Huệ (middle brother, future Emperor Quang Trung)

sources.name: Đại Việt Sử Ký Tục Biên (Continuation of the Complete Annals of Đại Việt)
George Dutton, The Tây Sơn Uprising: Society and Rebellion in Eighteenth-Century Vietnam (2006)
Li Tana, Nguyễn Cochinchina: Southern Vietnam in the Seventeenth and Eighteenth Centuries (1998)

events.certainty_note: The 1771 start date is established in Vietnamese historical sources, though the exact month of the initial uprising varies between accounts. The broader trajectory is well documented.

events.title: James Cook Returns from First Voyage of Discovery
events.description: On 12 July 1771, HMS Endeavour arrived back in England, completing Lieutenant James Cook's first voyage of Pacific exploration (1768–1771). The expedition had observed the transit of Venus from Tahiti, circumnavigated and charted the coastlines of New Zealand, and made the first European mapping of the east coast of Australia, which Cook claimed for Britain as New South Wales. The voyage also produced major botanical and zoological collections gathered by Joseph Banks and Daniel Solander, transforming European knowledge of Pacific natural history. Cook's charts set new standards of accuracy for Pacific navigation.
key_figures: James Cook (lieutenant, commander of HMS Endeavour)

sources.name: James Cook, Journal of HMS Endeavour, 1768–1771
Joseph Banks, The Endeavour Journal, 1768–1771
J.C. Beaglehole (ed.), The Journals of Captain James Cook on His Voyages of Discovery, vol. I (1955)

events.certainty_note: Exceptionally well documented through Cook's and Banks's journals, Admiralty records, and surviving charts. Return date is precise.

events.title: Completion of the First Edition of Encyclopaedia Britannica
events.description: The first edition of the Encyclopaedia Britannica was completed in 1771, having been issued in 100 weekly parts beginning in December 1768. Edited by William Smellie and published by Andrew Bell and Colin Macfarquhar, the three-volume work contained 2,659 pages with 160 copper-plate engravings. Unlike the French Encyclopédie's alphabetical essay format, the Britannica combined short dictionary entries with longer treatises on major subjects. It became the foundation for what would grow into the most influential English-language encyclopaedia, continuously published for over two centuries.

sources.name: Encyclopaedia Britannica, 1st edition, 3 vols. (Edinburgh, 1768–1771)
Herman Kogan, The Great EB: The Story of the Encyclopaedia Britannica (1958)

events.certainty_note: Surviving copies of the first edition confirm the publication timeline. The final volume's completion date in 1771 is well established.

events.title: Struensee's Reform Dictatorship in Denmark
events.description: Throughout 1771, Johann Friedrich Struensee, the German-born physician who had become the de facto ruler of Denmark through his influence over the mentally ill King Christian VII, issued over 1,000 cabinet orders implementing radical Enlightenment reforms. These included abolition of censorship, reform of the judiciary, abolition of noble privileges in civil service appointments, public health measures, and reorganisation of finances. His affair with Queen Caroline Matilda was an open secret. Struensee's pace of reform, his disregard for Danish national sentiment, and his personal conduct united conservative and nationalist opposition, leading to a palace coup and his arrest on 17 January 1772.
key_figures: Johann Friedrich Struensee (de facto regent, royal physician)

sources.name: Danish Royal Cabinet Orders (Kabinetsordrer), 1770–1772
Reverdil, Struensée et la cour de Copenhague, 1760–1772 (1858, memoir of Christian VII's tutor)
Ulrik Langen, The Ersatz Diplomat: Struensee and the Court of Copenhagen (in Scandinavian Journal of History, 2008)

events.certainty_note: Struensee's reforms are documented in surviving cabinet orders. The political narrative is well attested in Danish state archives and contemporary diplomatic correspondence.

events.title: Richard Arkwright Opens Cromford Mill
events.description: In 1771, Richard Arkwright opened his water-powered cotton spinning mill at Cromford in Derbyshire, marking a pivotal moment in the early Industrial Revolution. The mill used Arkwright's water frame spinning machine (patented 1769) to produce stronger cotton thread suitable for warp yarn, which had previously been imported or spun from linen. Cromford Mill is often cited as the first successful water-powered cotton mill and established the factory system model — centralised production with disciplined wage labour — that would transform British manufacturing and eventually global industry.

sources.name: Arkwright's patent specification for the water frame, 1769 (British Patent No. 931)
R.S. Fitton and A.P. Wadsworth, The Strutts and the Arkwrights, 1758–1830 (1958)
Robert Owen, The Life of Robert Owen (1857) — early factory system description

events.certainty_note: The mill building survives and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site (Derwent Valley Mills). Partnership records and patent documents establish the 1771 opening date, though some preparatory work began in 1770.

events.title: Ali Bey's Syrian Campaign from Egypt
events.description: In 1771, Ali Bey al-Kabir, the Mamluk strongman who had effectively declared Egyptian independence from the Ottoman Empire in 1769, sent his general Abu al-Dhahab to invade Ottoman Syria. The expedition captured Damascus in June 1771, briefly giving Ali Bey control over both Egypt and the Levant. However, Abu al-Dhahab unexpectedly withdrew from Damascus and soon turned against his master. Ali Bey's bid for an independent Egyptian-Syrian state represented the most serious Mamluk challenge to Ottoman authority in the eighteenth century, exploiting Ottoman distraction during the Russo-Turkish War.
key_figures: Ali Bey al-Kabir (Mamluk ruler of Egypt)
Zahir al-Umar (ruler of northern Palestine, Ali Bey's ally)

sources.name: Al-Jabarti, Aja'ib al-Athar fi'l-Tarajim wa'l-Akhbar (Chronicle of Egypt, early 19th century)
European diplomatic correspondence from Cairo and Constantinople, 1771
Thomas Philipp, Acre: The Rise and Fall of a Palestinian City, 1730–1831 (2001)

events.certainty_note: Documented in al-Jabarti's chronicle, European consular dispatches, and Ottoman records. The campaign's timeline is firmly established.

events.title: Gustav III Becomes King of Sweden
events.description: On 12 February 1771, King Adolf Frederick of Sweden died suddenly (reportedly from overeating), and his son succeeded as Gustav III. Gustav inherited a kingdom paralysed by factional strife between the Hat and Cap parties in the Riksdag, which had reduced royal power to a cipher during the 'Age of Liberty' (1718–1772). Cultivated, Francophile, and ambitious, Gustav immediately began plotting to restore royal authority. Within eighteen months he would execute a bloodless coup d'état (August 1772), ending the Age of Liberty and establishing an 'enlightened absolutism' that would define Swedish politics for two decades.
key_figures: Louisa Ulrika of Prussia (Queen Mother, Gustav's mother)

sources.name: Swedish Riksdag records and royal correspondence, 1771
H. Arnold Barton, Scandinavia in the Revolutionary Era, 1760–1815 (1986)

events.certainty_note: Thoroughly documented in Swedish state archives and contemporary diplomatic correspondence.

events.title: Sugita Genpaku Witnesses Anatomical Dissection at Kozukappara
events.description: On 4 March 1771 (3rd day of the 3rd month, Meiwa 8), the physician Sugita Genpaku and several colleagues attended the dissection of an executed criminal at the Kozukappara execution ground in Edo. Comparing the actual organs to illustrations in a Dutch anatomical text (Ontleedkundige Tafelen, a translation of Johann Adam Kulmus's Anatomische Tabellen), they were stunned by the accuracy of Western anatomy versus the errors in traditional Chinese-derived medical texts. This experience motivated Sugita and Maeno Ryōtaku to undertake a Japanese translation, published in 1774 as Kaitai Shinsho (New Book of Anatomy), a landmark in the rangaku (Dutch learning) movement.
key_figures: Maeno Ryōtaku (physician and Dutch language scholar)

sources.name: Sugita Genpaku, Rangaku Kotohajime (The Dawn of Western Science in Japan, 1815 memoir)
Kaitai Shinsho (New Book of Anatomy, 1774) — preface describing the 1771 dissection
Timon Screech, The Western Scientific Gaze and Popular Imagery in Later Edo Japan (1996)

events.certainty_note: The date is precisely recorded in Sugita's memoir Rangaku Kotohajime, written decades later but consistent with other contemporary sources. The event is a canonical moment in Japanese intellectual history.

events.title: Falkland Islands Crisis Resolution
events.region: Falkland Islands / Port Egmont, South Atlantic
events.description: In January 1771, Britain and Spain resolved the Falkland Islands crisis that had brought the two nations to the brink of war. Spain had expelled the British garrison from Port Egmont in June 1770. After intense diplomatic negotiations — with France declining to support Spain under the Family Compact — Spain agreed to restore Port Egmont to Britain in a declaration signed on 22 January 1771. However, the agreement included no Spanish renunciation of sovereignty, and a secret understanding reportedly allowed Britain to withdraw voluntarily later (which it did in 1774). The episode demonstrated British naval supremacy and France's unwillingness to fight over distant colonial disputes at that moment.
key_figures: Prince de Masserano (Spanish Ambassador to London)
Samuel Johnson (wrote pamphlet Thoughts on the Late Transactions Respecting Falkland's Islands, 1771)

sources.name: Anglo-Spanish Declaration of 22 January 1771 (diplomatic text)
Samuel Johnson, Thoughts on the Late Transactions Respecting Falkland's Islands (1771)
Julius Goebel, The Struggle for the Falkland Islands (1927, reissued 1982)

events.certainty_note: Diplomatic documents survive in both British and Spanish archives. The existence and terms of the alleged secret understanding remain debated.

events.title: Great Bengal Famine Continues
events.region: Bengal, British East India Company territory
events.description: The Great Bengal Famine of 1770 continued into 1771 with devastating mortality. Estimates suggest that approximately 10 million people — roughly one-third of Bengal's population — died between 1769 and 1773 from starvation and associated epidemics. The famine was triggered by crop failure from drought and flooding but was catastrophically worsened by East India Company revenue extraction policies, which maintained or increased tax demands even as the crisis deepened. The Company's failure to suspend revenue collection or provide relief is regarded as a defining indictment of early colonial economic governance in India.
key_figures: Warren Hastings (arrived as second-in-council, 1772; later Governor-General)
John Cartier (Governor of Bengal during the famine)
Muhammad Reza Khan (Naib Nazim, Bengali administrator under Company oversight)

sources.name: East India Company correspondence and revenue records, Bengal, 1770–1772
Reports to the House of Commons Select Committee on East India Company affairs, 1772–1773
Rajat Datta, Society, Economy and the Market: Commercialization in Rural Bengal, c. 1760–1800 (2000)

events.certainty_note: The famine is extensively documented in Company records and contemporary accounts. The death toll of ~10 million is widely cited but difficult to verify precisely; estimates range from 2 to 10 million depending on methodology and geographic scope.

events.title: Confederation of Bar — Final Stages in Poland
events.description: The Confederation of Bar (formed 1768), a Polish noble uprising against Russian domination and the reforms of King Stanisław August Poniatowski, continued its increasingly desperate resistance through 1771. On 3 November 1771, confederates attempted to kidnap King Stanisław August from his carriage in Warsaw — the plot failed and backfired diplomatically, discrediting the movement across Europe. Russian forces under Alexander Suvorov systematically reduced confederate strongholds. The confederation's failure paved the way for the First Partition of Poland in 1772, as Russia, Prussia, and Austria exploited the chaos to dismember Polish territory.
key_figures: Charles Dumouriez (French military advisor to the confederates)

sources.name: Stanisław August Poniatowski, memoir account of his kidnapping (contemporary correspondence)
Jerzy Łojek, The International Crisis of 1768–1774 (in Polish historical journals)
Robert Howard Lord, The Second Partition of Poland (1915) — includes analysis of the First Partition's origins

events.certainty_note: The kidnapping attempt is precisely documented. The broader military campaign is well attested in Russian, Polish, and French military and diplomatic archives.

events.title: Spanish Missions Established in Alta California
events.region: San Gabriel, Alta California (present-day Los Angeles area)
events.description: On 8 September 1771, Franciscan missionaries under Junípero Serra established Mission San Gabriel Arcángel, the fourth of the California missions, in what is now the Los Angeles metropolitan area. The mission system, begun with the founding of San Diego in 1769, was part of Spain's effort to secure Alta California against perceived Russian and British threats. The missions would fundamentally disrupt Indigenous Tongva and other Native Californian societies through forced conversion, labour coercion, and the introduction of European diseases, contributing to catastrophic population decline among California's indigenous peoples.
key_figures: Pedro Fages (military commander of Alta California)
Pedro Cambón and Ángel Somera (founding missionaries of San Gabriel)

sources.name: Junípero Serra, correspondence and mission reports, 1771
Francisco Palóu, Relación Histórica de la Vida del Venerable Padre Fray Junípero Serra (1787)
Steven W. Hackel, Children of Coyote, Missionaries of Saint Francis (2005)

events.certainty_note: Mission founding dates are precisely recorded in Franciscan mission registers and Serra's correspondence.

events.title: Publication of Tobias Smollett's Humphry Clinker
events.description: In June 1771, Tobias Smollett published The Expedition of Humphry Clinker, an epistolary novel widely regarded as his masterpiece and one of the finest comic novels of the eighteenth century. The novel follows the Welsh squire Matthew Bramble and his entourage on a tour of England and Scotland, offering satirical commentary on contemporary British society, politics, and manners. Published just months before Smollett's death in September 1771, the novel influenced later picaresque and epistolary fiction and provides historians with a vivid, if satirical, portrait of Georgian-era British life.

sources.name: Tobias Smollett, The Expedition of Humphry Clinker, 3 vols. (London, 1771)
Jeremy Lewis, Tobias Smollett (2003)

events.certainty_note: First edition copies survive; publication date is well established.

events.title: Nitrogen Identified by Daniel Rutherford
events.description: In 1771, Daniel Rutherford, a student of Joseph Black at the University of Edinburgh, conducted experiments that identified what he called 'noxious air' (nitrogen) as a distinct component of the atmosphere. By removing oxygen and carbon dioxide from a sealed air sample, he demonstrated that the remaining gas could not support combustion or life. Independently, Carl Wilhelm Scheele, Henry Cavendish, and Joseph Priestley were conducting related experiments around the same time. Rutherford's work, presented as his doctoral dissertation in September 1772, contributed to the dismantling of phlogiston theory and the emergence of modern chemistry.

sources.name: Daniel Rutherford, Dissertatio Inauguralis de Aere Fixo Dicto, aut Mephitico (Edinburgh, 1772 — reporting 1771 experiments)
J.R. Partington, A History of Chemistry, vol. III (1962)

events.certainty_note: The experiments were conducted in 1771 and reported in Rutherford's 1772 dissertation. Priority is sometimes disputed due to simultaneous independent work by Scheele, Cavendish, and Priestley.

events.title: Qianlong Emperor's Southern Inspection Tour
events.description: In early 1771, the Qianlong Emperor undertook his fourth Southern Inspection Tour (nanxun) through the prosperous Jiangnan region. These grand tours served multiple political functions: demonstrating imperial power, inspecting waterway infrastructure critical to grain transport, patronising local literati culture, and reinforcing Manchu-Han elite ties. The tours were enormously expensive, burdening local treasuries and populations despite the court's claims they imposed no additional levies. This tour occurred as the Qing Empire approached the zenith of its territorial extent but also faced mounting fiscal pressures that would intensify in subsequent decades.

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty) — Qianlong reign section
Michael G. Chang, A Court on Horseback: Imperial Touring and the Construction of Qing Rule, 1680–1785 (2007)

events.certainty_note: The Southern Inspection Tours are extensively documented in Qing court records. The fourth tour is sometimes dated to 1765 in some sources; the 1780 tour was the sixth. Some uncertainty exists in the exact year assignment — verification against Chinese historical calendars is advisable.

events.title: Kalmyk (Torghut) Migration to Qing Territory
events.region: Central Asian steppe — Volga River to Dzungaria (Xinjiang)
events.description: In January 1771, approximately 170,000 Torghut (Kalmyk) people under Ubashi Khan began an epic migration from the Volga steppe of the Russian Empire eastward toward their ancestral lands in Dzungaria, now under Qing control. Fleeing Russian encroachment on their pasturelands, military conscription, and Orthodox conversion pressure, they trekked over 3,000 miles across the Kazakh steppe over seven months. Roughly half perished from Kazakh raids, starvation, disease, and exposure before the survivors reached Qing territory in the Ili River valley in late summer 1771. The Qianlong Emperor resettled them, commemorating the event as a return of loyal subjects. The migration permanently ended the Kalmyk presence on the Volga.
key_figures: Catherine II (Russian Empress, unable to prevent the departure)

sources.name: Qianlong Emperor's commemorative inscription at Chengde (Tuěrhuětè Bù Quánbù Guī Shùn Jì, 1771)
Russian military and administrative reports on the Kalmyk departure, 1771
Michael Khodarkovsky, Where Two Worlds Met: The Russian State and the Kalmyk Nomads, 1600–1771 (1992)

events.certainty_note: One of the best-documented nomadic migrations in history, attested in both Russian and Qing archives plus the Qianlong Emperor's own inscriptions. Population figures and casualty estimates vary between sources but the event itself is beyond dispute.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Alamance (1771-003) is frequently described in popular American history as a 'precursor to the Revolution,' but modern scholarship (Kars 2002, Whittenburg 1977) challenges this framing. Many Regulators became Loyalists during the Revolutionary War, and their grievances were directed at local corruption rather than imperial authority — the teleological connection to 1776 is a retrospective construction. The death toll of the Great Bengal Famine (1771-013) is highly contested: the commonly cited figure of 10 million derives from early East India Company estimates and was propagated by later nationalist historiography, while recent demographic analysis (Datta 2000, Sen 1981) suggests the true figure may have been significantly lower, though still catastrophic. For the Qianlong Southern Tour (1771-018), some chronologies place the fourth tour in 1765 rather than 1771 (the 1780 tour being the sixth and final); the attribution here should be verified against the Qing Shilu calendar. The identification of nitrogen (1771-017) involves a priority dispute: Rutherford is conventionally credited, but Scheele may have independently identified the gas earlier, and Cavendish's unpublished work predates both.
historiographic_note: The year 1771 falls in a period of rich documentation for Europe, the Ottoman Empire, and East Asia, thanks to extensive state archives, diplomatic correspondence, and an active print culture. The main biases in the surviving record include: European-centered framing of global events (the Tây Sơn rebellion is far less studied in Western historiography than its significance warrants); colonial-era sources for India that reflect East India Company interests and may understate the Company's culpability in famine; Russian imperial sources that frame the Crimean conquest and Kalmyk affairs through the lens of civilisational progress; and a general European Enlightenment narrative that privileges scientific and intellectual achievements at the expense of the social conditions (slavery, colonialism, peasant immiseration) that enabled them. Sub-Saharan African, interior Southeast Asian, and Pacific Island histories for this year remain largely inaccessible through conventional European-language historiography.

graph_edges.note: Russian conquest of Crimea strengthened Russia's negotiating position, leading to favourable peace terms in 1774
Regulator movement was one of several colonial grievance episodes, though the direct causal link to the Revolution is debated
The 1771 Tây Sơn rebellion grew into the movement that unified Vietnam and defeated a Qing invasion in 1789
The success of Cook's first voyage led to his second expedition (1772–1775) to search for Terra Australis
Struensee's radical reforms provoked the conservative coup that led to his arrest and execution in 1772
Cromford Mill's success launched the factory system; Arkwright's patent was challenged and revoked in 1785, accelerating diffusion
Ali Bey exploited Ottoman distraction during the Russo-Turkish War to launch his Syrian campaign
The 1771 dissection directly motivated the translation project that produced Kaitai Shinsho in 1774
The Bengal famine's devastation contributed to Parliamentary scrutiny of the East India Company, resulting in the 1773 Regulating Act
The Confederation's failure and the chaos it produced gave Russia, Prussia, and Austria the pretext for the First Partition
The Qing conquest of Dzungaria in the 1750s created the territorial vacuum the Torghuts migrated into
Gustav III's accession in 1771 set the stage for his August 1772 coup restoring royal power

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration
